questions.question: What is the name of the school of thought founded by Siddhārtha Gautama?
questions.explanation: Siddhārtha Gautama was born into a royal family in modern-day Nepal and later became known as the Buddha.

questions.question: Who is considered the 'great hero' in Jainism?
questions.explanation: Prince Vardhamāna, also known as Mahāvīra, was born into a royal family and became a spiritual leader after achieving 'infinite knowledge'.

questions.question: What is the term for refraining from physical violence against a person or an animal?
questions.explanation: In Buddhism and Jainism, ahimsa means not only avoiding harm to others but also refraining from negative thoughts.

questions.question: What is the name of the community of monks and nuns founded by the Buddha?
questions.explanation: The Sangha was a community of bhikṣhus and, later, bhikṣhuṇīs who dedicated themselves to practising and spreading the Buddha's teachings.

questions.question: What is the name of the deity associated with fire in Vedic culture?
questions.explanation: Daily rituals were generally in the form of prayers and offerings to Agni, but those rituals became more complex over time.

questions.question: Who is considered the founder of Jainism?
questions.explanation: Prince Vardhamāna, also known as Mahāvīra, was born into a royal family and became a spiritual leader after achieving 'infinite knowledge'.

questions.question: What is the term for a group of families or clans sharing a tradition of common descent?
questions.explanation: In ancient India, tribes were not called by this name but were referred to as janas living in specific environments.

questions.question: How many tribes are recognized in the Constitution of India?
questions.explanation: According to official figures, in 2011 India had 705 tribes spread over most States, amounting to a population of about 104 million people.

questions.question: What is the term for teachings or practices transmitted through everyday practice without written texts?
questions.explanation: India has rich oral traditions, such as the Vedas, which were transmitted through generations before being written down.

questions.question: Who was the first Buddhist monk to found a community of monks and nuns?
questions.explanation: The Sangha was founded by the Buddha, who dedicated himself to practising and spreading his teachings.

important_notes.title: Vedic Culture
important_notes.content: Developed many rituals directed towards various deities for individual or collective benefit and wellbeing. Daily rituals were generally in the form of prayers and offerings to Agni, but those rituals became more complex over time.

important_notes.title: Buddhism
important_notes.content: Founded by Siddhārtha Gautama, also known as the Buddha. The Sangha was a community of monks and nuns who dedicated themselves to practising and spreading his teachings.

important_notes.title: Jainism
important_notes.content: Founded by Prince Vardhamāna, also known as Mahāvīra. Jain teachings include ahimsa, anekāntavāda, and aparigraha.

important_notes.title: Oral Traditions
important_notes.content: India has rich oral traditions, such as the Vedas, which were transmitted through generations before being written down.

important_notes.title: Tribe
important_notes.content: A group of families or clans sharing a tradition of common descent, culture, and language. In ancient India, tribes were not called by this name but were referred to as janas living in specific environments.

important_notes.title: Vedanta
important_notes.content: Everything — human life, nature, and the universe — is one divine essence called brahman or tat.

important_notes.title: Upaniṣhads
important_notes.content: A group of texts that built upon Vedic concepts and introduced new ones, such as rebirth and karma.

important_notes.title: Rebirth
important_notes.content: According to one school of thought, generally known as Vedanta, everything — human life, nature, and the universe — is one divine essence called brahman or tat.

important_notes.title: Karma
important_notes.content: Our actions or their results. According to one school of thought, generally known as Vedanta, everything — human life, nature, and the universe — is one divine essence called brahman or tat.

important_notes.title: Dharma
important_notes.content: A central concept in Hinduism and other Eastern religions, referring to one's duty or moral obligation towards oneself and others.

facts.fact: The word 'Jain' comes from jina, meaning 'conqueror', which refers to the conquest of ignorance and attachments, so as to reach enlightenment.
India has over 700 tribes spread across most States, amounting to a population of about 104 million people.
The Vedas were transmitted through generations before being written down, making India's oral traditions rich and complex.
Buddhism was founded by Siddhārtha Gautama, also known as the Buddha, who dedicated himself to practising and spreading his teachings.
Jainism was founded by Prince Vardhamāna, also known as Mahāvīra, who achieved 'infinite knowledge' after 12 years of ascetic discipline.
The Sangha, a community of monks and nuns, was founded by the Buddha to spread his teachings far and wide.
Vedanta is one school of thought that suggests everything — human life, nature, and the universe — is one divine essence called brahman or tat.
Upaniṣhads are a group of texts that built upon Vedic concepts and introduced new ones, such as rebirth and karma.
Rebirth and karma are central concepts in many Eastern religions, including Hinduism and Buddhism.
Dharma is one's duty or moral obligation towards oneself and others, a central concept in Hinduism and other Eastern religions.
